Q1: My study routine was honestly pretty chaotic. I'm 29, working odd jobs to make ends meet, so I couldn't stick to a neat schedule like younger students might. I'd usually carve out chunks of time whenever I could—early mornings before work, late nights when things quieted down. I tried to watch the video lectures in batches when I had a free afternoon, taking notes on paper since I don't have reliable internet at home. It wasn't organized in the traditional sense, but I made it work with what I had.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly depending on what was happening in my life. Some weeks I could put in solid hours, other weeks work would pile up or I'd be too exhausted to focus. Deadlines definitely pushed me to cram more, but I tried to stay somewhat consistent when possible. Being older, I've learned that rigid schedules don't survive contact with real life, so I adapted as needed.
Q3: Usually I'd log in when I knew there was new content posted or when an assignment deadline was approaching. Sometimes I'd get a notification reminder and that would prompt me to check in. I also logged in just to see if there were any discussion posts I should respond to, since I knew participation mattered. Without internet at home, I had to be intentional about when I could access the LMS at the library or a café.
Q4: Yeah, after the first few weeks I got into a rhythm of going to the public library on certain days specifically for coursework. That became my routine—Tuesday and Thursday afternoons were my LMS days. I'd download what I could, watch videos, participate in discussions, and then work offline the rest of the week. It wasn't automatic at first, but necessity made it a habit.
Q5: The video lectures were absolutely essential for me since I'm a visual learner. I could pause, rewind, and really absorb the material at my own pace. The discussion forums were also helpful because I could see how other students were thinking through problems. Sometimes reading someone else's question would clarify something I hadn't even realized I was confused about.
Q6: I tended to skip the optional readings and supplementary materials. With limited time and no internet at home, I had to prioritize ruthlessly. If something wasn't directly tied to an assignment or exam, it often got pushed aside. I also didn't engage much with some of the interactive quizzes because they felt more like busywork than actual learning for me.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to understanding the core concepts or completing assignments. I needed to feel like I was making progress, not just checking boxes. Given my situation, every hour I spent studying was time I wasn't earning money, so efficiency mattered a lot. If something helped me grasp a difficult concept or prepare for an exam, it was valuable.
Q8: Optional activities always felt like a luxury I couldn't afford. Required ones got my attention because they affected my grade directly. With optional stuff, I'd think 'that looks interesting' but then reality would set in—I had limited library time and assignments to complete. It's not that I didn't want to explore more; I just had to be strategic about where I put my energy.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my studying happened offline. I'd download lecture videos and PDFs when I was at the library, then study from those at home or during breaks at work. I also used a lot of handwritten notes and diagrams since that helps me remember things better. I bought a used textbook from a previous student because I knew I couldn't rely on always having internet access.
Q10: There were many nights where I'd be at my kitchen table with my downloaded notes and videos, working through problems for hours. None of that showed up anywhere in the system. I'd also draw concept maps and diagrams to visualize relationships between ideas—that's how my brain works best. The LMS only saw me when I was connected, which was maybe a third of my actual study time.
Q11: All the time. I'd watch a video once online, maybe twice if I could, but then I'd have it downloaded and would revisit it multiple times at home. Same with reading materials—I'd access them once to download, then spend hours with printed copies or saved PDFs. My actual engagement with the material was way deeper than my access logs would suggest.
Q12: There was a stretch mid-semester where my anxiety really spiked. I was worried about money, worried about failing, worried about whether going back to school at 29 was even the right choice. During that time, even logging in felt overwhelming. I'd sit at the library computer and just feel paralyzed, scrolling without really absorbing anything. The CBT sessions helped me work through some of that.
Q13: Definitely. When work got demanding or I was exhausted from juggling everything, my LMS usage dropped even if my studying didn't completely stop. Confusion was a big factor too—sometimes I'd log in, feel lost about where to start, and log out without accomplishing much. The fatigue was real; some days I just couldn't process new information no matter how hard I tried.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd be watching a video but my mind was completely elsewhere—thinking about bills, about work, about whether I was going to make it through this semester. My eyes were on the screen but nothing was sticking. I'd have to go back later and rewatch the same content. Those sessions probably looked like engagement in the data, but they weren't productive at all.
Q15: I postponed the bigger assignments more than I should have. The anxiety would build up around them, and I'd tell myself I needed to understand the material better first. But really, I was just scared of failing. It's a pattern I've noticed in myself—the more important something is, the more I tend to avoid it initially. The CBT work helped me recognize this and push through it somewhat.
Q16: Deadlines were my main driver, honestly. I'd ramp up my LMS activity significantly in the days before something was due. It wasn't ideal, but with my schedule, I often couldn't engage deeply until the pressure was on. I tried to at least download materials early so I could work on them offline, but the actual submission crunch always happened close to the deadline.
Q17: When I got feedback, it definitely affected my motivation. Positive comments made me feel like maybe I could do this after all. Critical feedback was harder—it would sometimes trigger my anxiety and make me avoid that subject for a bit. But I tried to use it constructively. The problem was feedback often came too late for me to really incorporate it into the next assignment.
Q18: The course structure was challenging for someone in my situation. Having 14 online courses meant a lot of content spread across different modules and timelines. I appreciated that things were organized, but the sheer volume was overwhelming. The structure assumed students had consistent access and time, which I didn't always have. I had to create my own meta-structure to keep track of everything.
Q19: The video content being downloadable was a lifesaver—that made engagement possible for me. But some interactive elements required constant internet connection, which was a barrier. The discussion forums had tight response windows that were hard to meet with my limited access times. A more flexible timeline for participation would have helped people like me engage more meaningfully.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me—spending a lot of time trying to understand the material, watching videos multiple times, reading discussions, but then struggling to translate that into completed assignments. Maybe they're dealing with anxiety or perfectionism, where they engage with content but freeze when it comes to producing work. Or they might be confused about what's actually expected in submissions.
Q21: That makes sense to me. If someone has good resources outside the LMS—textbooks, study groups, prior knowledge—they might not need to be online much to do well. I tried to be that person but my exam scores show I didn't quite manage it. Still, I know my understanding was better than my grades reflect. Sometimes the LMS is just one tool among many, not the whole picture.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when someone logs in, how long they spend, what they click on. That's useful for spotting students who've disappeared entirely. But it misses the quality of engagement, the offline work, the mental state of the student. It can't tell if someone's staring at a screen while panicking or deeply focused. It's like measuring presence without measuring learning.
Q23: Being engaged means actually wrestling with the ideas, not just going through the motions. It's when I'm drawing connections, asking questions, feeling curious or even frustrated because I want to understand. It's not about how many times I logged in—it's about whether my brain was actively working on the material. Some of my most engaged moments happened at my kitchen table with no internet in sight.
Q24: The data wouldn't show the hours I spent with downloaded materials, the concept maps I drew, the way I'd explain things to myself out loud while cooking dinner. It wouldn't show my anxiety spikes or how the CBT sessions helped me push through. It wouldn't capture that I'm a 29-year-old trying to change my life with limited resources, showing up despite everything working against me. The numbers just can't tell that story.
